claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 8 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To continue assessing your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask:

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to ask:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment. 

Does Alex live with you full-time, or do they spend time living with someone else (such as another parent or relative)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with me full-time at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any residential accommodation, or have they been for an extended period recently?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but they do live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I need to understand this situation a bit better as it may affect eligibility.

When you say Alex has been in care for a couple of months but also lives with you, could you clarify:

- Does Alex spend any nights at home with you each week, and if so, how many?
- Is the care arrangement through your local council?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in care through the local council, but they come home to stay with me on the weekends - so they do spend time at home each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. This is helpful information.

To be more specific, when Alex comes home on the weekends, how many nights does Alex typically spend at home with you each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They usually spend about 2 nights at home with me each week - they come home Friday evening and go back Sunday.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on the official rules I've reviewed, let me explain the situation:

**For Alex:**

Since Alex has been in care through the local council for more than 8 weeks, the standard rule is that you would no longer be entitled to Child Benefit. However, there is an important exception: you may still be eligible if Alex spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you.

You've mentioned that Alex comes home on weekends and spends 2 nights (Friday evening to Sunday) with you each week. This would typically amount to at least 24 hours per week spent at home, which would qualify for the exception to the 8-week rule.

Therefore, you should be eligible to continue receiving Child Benefit for Alex, as long as this regular weekend arrangement continues.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Even though Alex is in local council care, the fact that they spend at least 24 hours at home with you each week (coming home on weekends) means you qualify for the exception to the normal 8-week rule. As long as Alex continues to come home regularly like this, you should be able to continue receiving Child Benefit.

If your circumstances change (for example, if Alex stops coming home for weekends), you should contact HMRC to update them. Also, please note that if either you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit itself.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for them. Although Alex has been in care through the local council for more than 8 weeks (which would normally end Child Benefit entitlement after 8 weeks), there is an exception that applies. According to the rules, for children in care, you can still receive Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Alex comes home every weekend, spending 2 nights (Friday evening to Sunday) with the claimant each week, which amounts to at least 24 hours per week at home. This meets the exception criteria, so the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 8-year-old child Alex, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is in local council care for more than 8 weeks, Alex regularly spends weekends at home (2 nights per week), which satisfies the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child in care spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

According to the rules, for children in care, you can still receive Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Alex comes home every weekend, spending 2 nights (Friday evening to Sunday) with the claimant each week, which amounts to at least 24 hours per week at home. This meets the exception criteria, so the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 8-year-old child Alex, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is in local council care for more than 8 weeks, Alex regularly spends weekends at home (2 nights per week), which satisfies the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child in care spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
